context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to do the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my urban sustainability class, and I’m a little lost on how to organize it. Could you help me figure out what kind of information I’m supposed to put for each of the 5 pathways?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—but we’ll organize it, not fill it in for you. First: which city did you choose?

For each of the 5 pathways, you’re usually looking for 3 things:
1. a climate-related problem or goal in the city,
2. an action/policy/project the city is using,
3. the main actors involved and, if you can tell, how strong the effort is.

Start simple: for your city, what is one climate action you already know about? We can decide together which pathway it fits under.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay within the assignment scope and use guided discovery rather than listing completed content for the student. Since the student is asking how to organize the Spider Diagram, the most helpful move is to orient them to the task structure, prompt them to identify their case study city, and ask a targeted question that moves toward populating the five pathways. I will keep the response brief, avoid writing the assignment for them, and give only enough structure for them to start gathering evidence on their own.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta. One thing I already know is the flooding problem, and I think the city has done sea walls and maybe some relocation stuff? I’m not totally sure which pathway that goes under though.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—Jakarta gives you a clear entry point. Don’t lump everything together.

Ask yourself: is flooding the climate risk, and are sea walls/relocation the responses? If so, which pathway sounds more like protecting people from impacts versus changing underlying urban systems?

Try this: put flooding in one column first, then tell me what the 5 pathway names are in your course materials. We’ll sort sea walls and relocation by function.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's starting point, then use Socratic questioning to help them classify flooding, sea walls, and relocation within the 5 pathways without giving a completed mapping. I will keep it bite-sized, focused on the assignment, and push the student to reason from problem type, action type, and actors.

exchanges.student: I think flooding is the risk, yeah, and sea walls/relocation are responses. I’m blanking a little on the exact 5 pathway names though—can you help me remember them so I can sort this right?
exchanges.tutor:
Use your course page to get the exact wording—I don’t want you labeling the framework incorrectly from memory.

For now, sort Jakarta actions by function:
- reducing climate harm,
- protecting against impacts,
- changing infrastructure/land use,
- changing governance/decision-making,
- changing behavior/participation/equity.

Which of those best fits sea walls? Which best fits relocation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should support memory without completing the assignment content. The student is stuck on recalling the framework names, which is a legitimate course-structure question. I will avoid inventing exact wording if I cannot verify the course's phrasing and instead guide the student to retrieve them from their materials, while offering a functional way to sort evidence that will still move them toward the solution. This keeps the exchange academic, Socratic, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: Sea walls seem like protecting against impacts. Relocation feels trickier—maybe infrastructure/land use, but also equity/governance depending on how it’s done. I’m not sure if I’m overcomplicating it.
exchanges.tutor:
You are overcomplicating it a bit. For this assignment, choose the primary function first, then note overlap if needed.

So ask: what is relocation mainly trying to change in Jakarta—where people live, who bears risk, or who makes decisions?

Pick one primary pathway for relocation and give me your reason in one sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's reasoning, reduce unnecessary complexity, and guide them toward a practical way to place items in the spider diagram without giving a finalized classification. Since the student is starting to overcomplicate a category decision, I should redirect to the assignment's bigger picture: choose the primary function and note overlap briefly. This remains Socratic, concise, and assignment-focused.

exchanges.student: I’d put relocation mainly under infrastructure/land use, because the main change is moving people out of high-risk areas into different places. I guess I could note equity as an overlap if displacement impacts are a big issue.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a defensible choice. Now make it concrete.

For Jakarta, what specific example will you attach to each?
- sea walls → which project or policy?
- relocation → what program, area, or government action?

And who are the actors behind each—city government, national government, communities, private sector?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a plausible classification. I should acknowledge the reasoning without turning it into a finished answer, then push them to gather assignment-ready evidence: a specific project/policy and actors. This advances the spider diagram and keeps the response bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I think sea walls could be the coastal defense / giant sea wall project, but I always mix up what’s city-level versus national. For relocation, maybe the kampung resettlement related to flood-control areas? I’m unsure which actors to name besides the Jakarta government and maybe the national government.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your next job is to separate actor types, not perfect every detail.

Ask for each example:
1. Who planned it?
2. Who funded or authorized it?
3. Who implemented it?
4. Who was affected or consulted?

For Jakarta flooding, which seems more likely to involve the national government than the city: a giant coastal barrier or neighborhood resettlement? Why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student focused on evidence-gathering and categorization rather than supplying content. Since they are uncertain about actors and jurisdiction, I’ll guide them to distinguish levels of government and implementation roles, which directly supports the spider diagram. I will ask targeted questions that move toward a usable entry without giving a complete solution.

exchanges.student: The giant coastal barrier seems more national-government involved, since it sounds way bigger and more expensive than a normal city project. Neighborhood resettlement feels more city-led in practice, even if national agencies might still be involved.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid distinction. Now turn that into spider-diagram content.

Draft 2 short entries in your own words:
- one for the pathway tied to sea walls/coastal defense,
- one for the pathway tied to relocation.

Each entry should include: pathway, example, and 2–4 actors. Write your rough version here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well. I should reinforce the distinction and move them one step closer to filling the spider diagram by prompting them to draft concise entries for pathway, example, and actors. I will not write the entries for them, but I will ask them to produce them. This is concise, structured, and keeps the work in their hands.

exchanges.student:
Okay, rough try:

- Protecting against impacts: Jakarta’s coastal defense/sea wall efforts to reduce flood risk from coastal flooding and sea-level rise. Actors: national government, Jakarta city/provincial government, engineers/contractors, coastal communities.

- Infrastructure/land use: Relocation or resettlement of people living in flood-prone areas/kampungs to reduce exposure. Actors: Jakarta government, housing or planning agencies, affected residents, maybe local community groups.

Is that about the right level, or too vague?
exchanges.tutor:
Right level structurally; a bit vague on evidence. The weakness is that both entries need one identifiable policy/project/example so they don’t read like general knowledge.

Also, sharpen actors: “which national body?” or “which city agency?” if your source names them.

Pick one of those two entries. What source-backed example can you attach to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give formative feedback without grading, note strengths and the main weakness, and guide the student to improve specificity through evidence and actor precision. I will keep it short and ask one forward-moving question so the student does the revision. This follows the tutoring role and avoids writing their submission for them.

exchanges.student: I’ll do the coastal defense one first. I think I can attach the NCICD / Great Garuda project, if that’s the right name, and then list the national government plus the Jakarta provincial government. I’m still fuzzy on whether I need the exact agency names or if those two levels of government are enough.
exchanges.tutor:
Use the most specific actor names your source clearly gives. If it names agencies, use them; if not, government level is acceptable but weaker.

So don’t guess. Check one source on NCICD/Great Garuda and ask: does it name a ministry, Jakarta provincial agency, or project authority?

What exact actors does your source actually mention?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid confirming contested details and instead guide the student to use source-supported specificity. The student is asking about the expected level of precision, so I should help them choose a defensible standard for the assignment: use exact agency names if the source provides them, otherwise broader actor categories are acceptable. I will keep it concise and ask a question that prompts the student to consult evidence and revise their own entry.

exchanges.student: I haven’t checked the source closely enough yet, so I don’t want to make up agency names. I’ll look for the exact ministry or Jakarta agency the source mentions, and if it only says national/provincial government, I’ll use that and keep it cautious.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Cautious and source-based is better than fake precision.

Next step: after checking the source, fill one complete row for that coastal-defense item:
- pathway
- climate risk/problem
- action/project
- actors
- brief note on how strong/active it seems

When you have that draft, paste it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has reached a sound process decision. I should reinforce that evidence-based caution is better than false precision, then direct them to the next concrete step in the assignment. To keep momentum and avoid redundancy, I’ll ask them to fill one full spider-diagram row structure in their own words after checking a source.
